segments.s593-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a past-tense clause; ایرانیان is the plural subject 'the Iranians'; آگهی is the direct object 'news/information'; یافتند is the past 3rd-person-plural of یافتن 'to find/obtain' used here as 'learned/received'. Word order is conjunction + subject + object + verb, a common Persian pattern for temporal clauses.
segments.s593-g2: یکایک is an adverbial 'one by one' / 'each'; سوی introduces a directional phrase 'toward'; چاره is the goal 'a remedy/solution'; بشتافتند is past 3rd-person-plural 'they hurried/rushed'. Together this is an adverbial + prepositional phrase + verb: 'each hurried toward a remedy.'
segments.s593-g3: چو again = 'when' introducing the clause; گشتند is past 3rd-plural of گشتن used here as 'they became' (change of state); زان = ز + آن 'from that' marking cause; رنج = 'suffering/hardship' as the source; یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/utterly'; ستوه is a predicative adjective 'weary' that complements گشتند ('they became weary'). The clause literal order is conjunction + verb + cause phrase + adverb + predicate.
segments.s593-g4: نشستند is the past 3rd-plural of نشستن 'they sat down'; یک با دگر is an idiomatic phrase 'one with another' (= together, mutually); همگروه is a noun/adjective 'of the same group' / 'companions' qualifying how they sat. Word order: verb then a circumstantial expression describing accompaniment.
segments.s593-g5: که here introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause 'that' or 'for'; این کار = 'this deed/affair' is the clause's subject; ز اندازه اندر گذشت is a fixed poetic expression meaning 'passed beyond measure' or 'exceeded the bounds' (ز = 'from/of', اندازه = 'measure/limit', اندر = 'in/within' combined with گذشت 'passed' to yield 'went beyond'). گذشت is past 'passed/exceeded'.
segments.s593-g6: This is a coordinated list introduced by repeated poetic prepositions: ز روم و ز هند = 'from Rum and from Hind/India'; و سواران دشت adds 'and the horsemen of the plain' (سواران = 'horsemen/riders', دشت = 'plain') — a terse, enumerative phrase listing peoples/forces.
segments.s593-g7: یکی چاره is 'one (a certain) remedy' or 'some solution'; باید is the impersonal/modal 'must/it is necessary'; کنون 'now' is a temporal adverb; ساختن is the infinitive 'to make/devise'. The structure is modal (باید) + infinitive (ساختن) with the noun phrase preceding to indicate what must be done.
segments.s593-g8: دل و جان = 'heart and life' (objects expressing total devotion); ازین کار literally 'from/for this work' (ازین = از + این, here meaning 'to/for this'); پرداختن is the infinitive meaning 'to attend to / to devote oneself to' (here: to expend/devote heart and life on the matter). The whole phrase functions as the intended remedy: 'to devote heart and life to this matter.'
segments.s593-g17: نگهدار is used as a predicative/title (an optative vocative sense: 'be/you be guardian') with an implied subject 'you' (copula 'باش' is omitted, typical of classical Persian). ایران is the noun 'Iran' governed by نگهدار ('guardian of Iran'). نیران is a poetic noun ('ardour/energy') linked with توی that follows and describes something located in/within you.
segments.s593-g18: به هر جای is a locative phrase 'in/every place'. پشت here means 'support/protection' (not merely 'back') and governs دلیران ('the brave' used as a plural substantive). توی follows as a locative/adverbial 'among' (i.e. 'be the backing among the brave in every place'). Word order is poetic: predicate ('پشت') + possessor/group ('دلیران') + locative 'توی'.
segments.s593-g19: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. این تخت is the subject 'this throne'. بی‌شاه و بی‌تاج are adjectival phrases 'kingless and uncrowned' describing تخت. شد is the past-tense copular/transformative verb 'became'. The whole segment: 'When this throne became kingless and uncrowned.'
segments.s593-g20: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'/'because of'. خون here denotes the bloodshed caused by battle. مرز is the subject 'the frontier'. چون = 'like/as'. پر is used in the sense 'full (of)' or as part of a simile (here 'like a plume/feather'). دراج is a poetic noun 'rider/runner' that combines with پر to form an image ('a rider's plume' or 'like a rider's feathered plume'). شد is the past 'became'.
segments.s593-g21: تو is the vocative/emphatic 'you'. گفتیم is the speaker's verb 'we said/proclaimed' (the lexicon entry given does not list the verbal sense; here it functions as 'we declared'). باشی is an optative/subjunctive form 'may you be' or 'be (you)'. خداوند مرز is a title 'lord/sovereign of the frontier' (خداوند used as honorific + مرز as its domain). The construction is elliptical but means 'We declare that you be lord of the frontier.'
segments.s593-g22: که introduces a clause giving the reason ('for'/'that'). این مرز را is the topicalized/direct-object-marked phrase 'this border' (را marking definiteness/emphasis). از تو here expresses cause/relation 'because of you' or 'from you'. دیدیم is the past 'we saw' (we perceived/considered). ارز is a nominal 'value/worth' — the phrase literally reads 'that we have seen this border's worth from you.'
segments.s593-g23: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. غارت is a noun 'plunder/pillage'. از here is the preposition 'from' (but in context it combines with تست — an archaic form of 'تو است' — to mean 'comes from you' or 'is your doing'). تست is the contracted/archaic copular phrase 'تو است' ('it is you' / 'comes from you') rather than the lexical 'test' meaning; the provided senses do not include this, so it's irregular/archaic. و links the next nominal phrase خون ریختن 'the spilling of blood' (ریختن is the infinitive/gerund 'to spill').
segments.s593-g24: به هر جای is a locative phrase 'in/at every place'. تاراج و آویختن are coordinated gerunds/nouns 'pillage and hanging' (آویختن here means 'to hang [people]' — the act of execution or strung-up bodies). The sequence lists the violent consequences now occurring everywhere.
segments.s593-g49: همی is a durative/adverbial particle meaning 'continually/keeps'; می‌چکد is present-tense imperfective 'it drips/keeps dripping' with impersonal subject left implied. گویی here is a poetic particle meaning 'as if' (introducing a simile) though that sense was not listed in the provided senses. از + روی + اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from her face' where اوی is an enclitic pronoun marking possession ('her'). Word order is verb (می‌چکد) before the simile phrase.
segments.s593-g50: همی again marks continuous action 'continually'; بوی مشک آید is an impersonal or simple present clause 'the scent of musk comes/rises' with آید (present of آمدن) as verb. از موی اوی is another from‑phrase 'from her hair' where اوی is the possessive enclitic 'her'. The two clauses are coordinated and describe continuous emanation.
segments.s593-g51: سخن‌گوی is a noun 'the speaker' (agent). بی‌فر و بی‌هوش are two predicative adjectives joined by و meaning 'without rank/glory and without consciousness/dazed'; گشت (from گشتن) is a past/resultative 'became' marking change of state. Word order is noun + predicate + verb, typical in older/formal Persian.
segments.s593-g52: پیامش is پیام + ـش (his/its message) with the enclitic ـش marking possession. سراسر here is adverbial/qualifying 'entirely/throughout' modifying فراموش گشت, where فراموش functions as 'forgotten' and گشت is the resultant past 'became/was'. The whole clause means 'his message became entirely forgotten.'
segments.s593-g53: بدانست is a past-tense verbal form 'he perceived/knew it.' The name بهرام is the logical subject. کو here is an interrogative/relative particle often meaning 'where' or 'that/that which' in older poetry; in this clause it helps introduce the subordinate idea and is idiomatic. خیره شد is a compound predicate 'became transfixed/stared' (خیره = staring/astonished, شد = became). Word order places the verb after the predicate, a common classical arrangement.
segments.s593-g54: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from/because of'; دیدار here denotes the sight/vision (used poetically as 'the sight/meeting') — this sense was not listed exactly among the provided senses, so it is treated as the perceptual noun 'sight'. چشم و دلش is a coordinated subject 'his eye and his heart' (دلش = دل + enclitic ـش). تیره شد is 'grew dark/was darkened' (تیره = dark, شد = became), indicating the emotional/visual effect of the sight.
segments.s593-g55: بپرسید is past 'he asked' (often used to mean 'he questioned'); بسیار here modifies the verb as 'much' or 'often' (i.e., 'asked many questions'). و is the coordinating 'and'. بنواختش (نواختن with enclitic ـش) can mean 'struck him' or 'struck (at) him' in context; it is a past tense form with the object enclitic attached.
segments.s593-g56: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase 'well/kindly' (به used to form the adverbial phrase + خوبی 'goodness/kindness'). بر تخت بنشاختش is a verbal phrase 'he set/placed him upon the throne' where بر تخت = 'on the throne' and بنشاختش (from بنشاختن) is a causative/resultative verb meaning 'caused to sit/placed' with the object enclitic ـش 'him'. The sequence gives the manner and result: kindly, he seated him on the throne.
segments.s593-g81: جوانوی روی شهنشاه = a noun phrase where جوانوی (an adjective/possessive-form meaning 'youthful' or 'of a young man') modifies روی ('face'); شهنشاه is the possessor 'the king/emperor'; دید is the simple past 'saw' (third-person). Word order is emphatic/poetic: 'he saw the youthful face of the shahanshah.'
segments.s593-g82: وزو نیز چندی سخنها شنید = وزو here is a contracted/poetic string meaning 'و ز او' ('and from him'); this specific lexical item is not supplied in the sense list, so mark it as unmatched. نیز is an adverb 'also'; چندی is a quantifier 'some/a few' (here modifying 'words'); سخنها is the plural definite/indefinite 'words/speeches'; شنید is the past 'heard'. The phrase thus means 'and also heard from him some words.'
segments.s593-g83: بپرسید is a past tense (classical narrative) 'he asked' or 'he inquired'. تا شاید introduces a purpose/hope clause ('so that/perhaps'); تا + شاید expresses 'in order that maybe' or 'so that perhaps'. او is the third-person pronoun 'he' (the potential beneficiary), and تخت را is the direct object 'the throne' with the accusative/definite marker را. The verb for 'obtain' is omitted and understood from context (a common poetic ellipsis).
segments.s593-g84: This segment is a coordination of three abstract nouns that share the same object marker را: بزرگی 'greatness', پیروزی 'victory', and بخت 'fortune/luck'. The final را marks them as definite direct objects of the implied verb ('to obtain' from the previous clause).
segments.s593-g85: ز منذر چو بشنید = a temporal clause: ز (poetic 'from') marks source 'from Munẓir'; چو بشنید 'when he heard' (چو = 'when', بشنید = past 'he heard'). زان‌سان is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying سخن 'words/speech'. The clause order is literary; literally 'from Munẓir when he heard thus words.'
segments.s593-g86: یکی روشن اندیشه افگند بن = a compact, poetic clause. یکی 'one/a single' quantifies the noun phrase روشن اندیشه 'clear/bright thought' (adjective after noun is normal). افگند is the past 'he threw/cast' used metaphorically; بن 'root/foundation' here is literal but used metaphorically as the target where the thought is planted or laid down. Together: 'he cast/laid down one clear thought as a foundation (i.e. formed a single clear resolve/plan).'
segments.s593-g87: چنین داد پاسخ = 'thus he gave an answer' (چنین 'thus', داد past 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'). The following که introduces the reported speech; که‌ای is the clause-introducer plus the vocative particle ای addressing the addressee. سرفراز is a vocative adjective 'O proud/victorious/exalted one.' In other words: 'He replied thus: "O exalted one..."'.
segments.s593-g88: به دانایی از هرکسی بی‌نیاز = a verbless predicative description in the quoted speech. به دانایی 'in respect of knowledge/wisdom' (به + noun), از هرکسی 'from/of everyone' and بی‌نیاز is an adjectival predicate 'needless/self-sufficient'. The construction means '(you are) in wisdom independent of everyone' or more naturally 'in wisdom you need no one.'
segments.s593-g89: This clause begins with the preposition از (’from, among’) followed by ایرانیان‌گر, here functioning as a conditional 'if (from) the Iranians…' — the classical particle گر attached to ایرانیان makes an if-clause ('if it is/from the Iranians'). خرد (wisdom) is the theme; گشته شد is a compound verbal phrase where گشته is a past participle/`become` form and شد is the auxiliary, producing a passive/perfect sense 'was become/was obtained' or 'was found'.
segments.s593-g90: فراوان is an adverb/adjective 'many, plentifully' modifying the following phrase. از آزادگان is a partitive/genitive-like construction 'of/from the freemen/nobles' (آزادگان = the free, nobles). کشته شد uses کشته (past participle 'killed') plus the auxiliary شد to form a passive 'were killed'. The clause thus states that many of the freemen were slain.
segments.s593-g91: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. من یکی 'I, one/a certain' is a topical subject (یکی = 'a certain' or 'one of'); نامجویم is the poet’s descriptive predicate formed from نامجو (name‑seeking, fame-seeker) with first-person ending -م (I am a seeker of fame). کهن 'old/ancient' modifies the speaker (an epithet appended for meter). Word order is topical: time → subject → predicate → epithet.
segments.s593-g92: اگر introduces a condition 'if'. بشنوی is the present/subjunctive 2nd‑person singular of شنیدن ('you hear/you listen') used in the protasis 'if you hear'. تا here links purpose/result and is followed by بگویم, the present/subjunctive 1st‑person of گفتن 'that I may tell' (i.e. 'I will tell' or 'let me tell'), and سخن is the object 'a tale/speech'. The construction 'اگر ... تا بگویم' = 'if you will listen, that I may tell (a tale)'.
segments.s593-g93: ترا is the object form of تو (here preposed as topic/emphatic: 'you'), represented in the token split as ت + را; با is the preposition 'with'. شهنشاه بهرام گور is a titled name phrase 'the king Bahram Gūr' (گور here is the epithet Gūr, part of the name). The group functions as an adjunct indicating accompaniment or association: 'you with King Bahram Gūr...'.
segments.s593-g94: خرامید is a past/preterital form of خرامیدن 'to stride/prance', but here is placed before باید (inverted for metrical/style reasons) so that باید ('must/ought') makes the predicate modal: 'must stride'. ابی ('azure/blue') qualifies جنگ ('war/battle') and شور ('ardor, tumult') is conjoined by و; together جنگ و شور is a two‑term object/description: 'azure war and ardor'. The clause states obligation/necessity: 'must stride with blue war and ardor.'
segments.s593-g95: به ایران‌زمین 'to/in the land of Iran' is a locative phrase setting place. در introduces an in/within phrase; ابا here is a classical noun (a kind of outer garment/robe used in hunting contexts) and is followed by یوز و باز 'cheetah and hawk' — animals associated with royal hunting. The cluster describes the king’s hunting accoutrements/location: 'in Iran, in his aba with cheetah and falcon.'
segments.s593-g96: چنانچون 'just as / such as' introduces a simile/comparison. بود is the past tense 'was'. شاه is the noun 'king', and گردن‌فراز is a compound epithet 'neck‑uplifting' → 'proud, haughty' describing the king. Together: 'just as the proud king was.'
segments.s593-g97: شنیدن is a verbal noun (masdar) meaning 'hearing' and functions as the subject of the clause; سخنهای is the plural genitive 'the words/speeches of…' with the ezāfe omitted in the poetic line; ایرانیان is the genitive/possessive noun 'the Iranians' modifying سخنهای.
segments.s593-g98: همانا is an emphatic particle 'truly/verily'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; جنبش is a noun 'movement/commotion' meaning 'agitation' here; نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'should not/ought not' indicating that harm must not occur; زیان is 'harm/detriment' and is the predicate of the impersonal construction.
segments.s593-g99: بگویی is a jussive/subjunctive second‑person form 'you should say' (an exhortation); the subject تو follows the verb (common in poetic word order); نیز is the adverb 'also'; آنچ is a relative 'that which/whatever'; اندرخورد is an adjective/noun meaning 'fitting, appropriate' that qualifies آنچ, so the phrase means 'say whatever is fitting.'
segments.s593-g100: خردمندی is an abstract noun 'wisdom/prudence'; و is the coordinating 'and'; دوری here means 'avoidance' (keeping at a distance); از is the preposition 'from/of'; بی‌خرد is an adjective/noun 'the foolish' or 'folly', so the phrase names what is fitting to be said—wisdom and avoidance of fools.
segments.s593-g101: ز رای means 'from/from the counsel' (ز + رای = 'from opinion/counsel'); the following ب + دان is an archaic/poetic sequence (here functioning to point to 'that' or to mark the object of reference), and دور داری منش literally 'you keep/maintain a distance in conduct'—دور داری is 'maintain/be distant' and منش is 'demeanor/character'. The whole segment instructs to keep one's conduct distant from that counsel.
segments.s593-g102: بپیچی is an imperative/subjunctive 'turn aside/avoid' (پیچیدن → to turn); ز is the preposition 'from'; بیغاره is an archaic/obscure word here (the exact nuance is archaic and context‑bound); و is 'and'; سرزنش is a noun 'reproach/blame' — together: 'turn away from (بیغاره) and reproach.'
segments.s593-g103: چو is a conjunction 'when'; بشنید is the past form 'he heard' (the prefixed b‑ is an older/poetic past/present marker here used for third person); منذر is a proper name (Munḏir) and appears after the verb by poetic inversion; ورا is the dative clitic 'to him'; هدیه is 'a gift'; داد is the past 'gave'—so: 'When Munḏir heard, he gave him a gift.'
segments.s593-g104: کسی is an indefinite subject 'someone'; کردش is کرد + انضمامِ ضمیرِ ش ('did/made him') — کردن used causatively 'to make (someone X)'; از شهر آباد is a prepositional phrase 'from the prosperous/inhabited city' modifying کسی; شاد is the predicate adjective 'glad/joyful', so 'Someone from the prosperous city made him glad.'
segments.s593-g33: جوانوی دانا is a noun phrase meaning “the (young) man who is wise”; جوانوی is an archaic/poetic adjectival form meaning “young (man-like)” followed by the adjective دانا “wise.” ز پیش introduces a prepositional phrase “from before/in front of,” so ز + پیش + سران = “from before the chiefs.” Word order is adjective after noun in the compound and the prepositional phrase follows to mark origin/place.
segments.s593-g34: بیامد is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb “he came.” سوی + noun marks direction (“toward”); دشت نیزه‌وران is a genitival or adjectival juxtaposition meaning “plain of the spearmen” (دشت modified by نیزه‌وران). The verb follows the subject phrase in this poetic line.
segments.s593-g35: به + name (منذر) marks the indirect object “to/for Munḏir.” سخن گفت is a common collocation meaning “spoke” (سخن as the content + گفت ‘said’). و links the next clause; نامه بداد is an archaic/poetic expression meaning “(he) gave a letter” — بداد here is the older poetic past form related to دادن. (Note: some lemma senses listed in the word data are nouns; the line uses verbal/poetic forms.)
segments.s593-g36: سخنهای ایرانیان is a genitive phrase “the words/speeches of the Iranians.” کرد یاد is a light-verb construction: کرد (to do/make) + یاد (remembrance/mention) = “he made mention of / recalled.” The verb کرد completes the action on the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s593-g37: سخنهایش (his words) appears before the verb for emphasis; بشنید is the past 3rd-person “he heard.” شاهِ عرب (the Arab king) is the subject, placed after the verb in the poetic word order. The whole clause reads “The Arab king heard his words.”
segments.s593-g38: به پاسخ = “to/for an answer” (direction/purpose). برو here is poetic diction meaning roughly “go/come toward” used with به پاسخ to express “in order to answer” or “to answer.” هیچ نگشاد لب is a negative statement: هیچ “at all/never,” نگشاد (an archaic negative past form of ‘to open’) = “did not open,” and لب “lip(s).” Together: “to answer he did not open his lips at all.”
segments.s593-g39: چنین گفت is a stock narrative formula “thus (he) said.” کای is a vocative particle “O,” addressing someone directly; دانشی چاره‌جوی are two epithets placed after the vocative — lit. “knowledgeable, seeker-of-remedy” — forming the addressee’s description. The sequence is a direct address introduced by the reporting formula.
segments.s593-g40: سخن زین نشان is the object of the imperative: سخن “words/message” modified by زین نشان “by/with this sign/token” (zīn here functions poetically as “of/from this / with this token”). با شهنشاه means “to/with the emperor (Shahanshah)” — با marking the recipient/presence — and گوی is an imperative-like verbal stem “say/speak” (built from the root ‘گوی’ meaning ‘saying’). Together: “Speak this message, with this token, to the Shahanshah.”
segments.s593-g25: نبودی is the past copula with negative prefix (literally 'was not'); in classical Persian verb-first order the predicate precedes the subject here. ازین پیش (از + این) is a temporal/adverbial phrase 'before this' placed after the verb; تو is the subject pronoun ('you') and بدکنش is a predicate noun 'evildoer' describing تو. The whole segment reads literally: 'was-not before-this you evildoer.'
segments.s593-g26: ز نفرین is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the curse' (ز = from/of). بترسیدی is a past/preterite second‑person form meaning 'you feared'; the following و سرزنش links a second feared item by coordination ('and reproach'), the verb understood from the preceding clause (ellipsis of the verb for the second object).
segments.s593-g27: نگه کن is an imperative 'look/attend!'. بدین here is a poetically contracted phrase corresponding to به + این (i.e. 'to/at these'); تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'until') and پسند آیدت is a fixed expression 'it pleases you' (آیدت = 'it comes to you' used idiomatically). The clause means 'Look to these so that they please you.'
segments.s593-g28: به پیران is a dative phrase 'to/for the elders' (به = 'to/for', پیران = 'elders'). سودمند آیدت is a predicate phrase 'will prove useful to you' (آیدت = 'it comes to you' = 'befall/benefit you'). سر این connects these ideas idiomatically — here introducing the respect/stance of the elders (literally 'in the elders' regard/for this'), so the segment reads as 'in the elders' estimation, this will be beneficial to you.'
segments.s593-g29: جز از تو is an exception phrase 'except for you'. زبر داوری is a noun phrase in which زبر means 'upper/superior' modifying داوری ('judgment'); دیگرست is the copula 'is different/other.' Together: 'Except for you, there is a different (or superior) judgment.'
segments.s593-g30: کز is the relative/comparative 'who/whose/that'. اندیشه is 'thought' and برترست is the predicate 'is superior'. برتران here is a plural substantive 'the superior ones' (comparative/relative target). The clause says 'whose thought is superior even to the superior ones' — i.e. his mind surpasses the best.
segments.s593-g31: بگوید here functions as a narrative verb 'says/said' in an older/classical present-used-for-past form (the text uses this historic-present style); فرستاده is the agent 'the messenger'. چیزی که دید is a relative clause 'something that he saw' (که introduces the relative clause; دید is simple past 'he saw'). The whole: 'the messenger said something that he saw.'
segments.s593-g32: سخن نیز 'also words' (= 'also what was said'), followed by a relative clause introduced by کز ('which/from which') and the source کاردانان شنید 'he heard (them) from the experts' (شنید = past 'heard'). The verb is understood to govern both the seen and the heard material: 'and also what he heard from the experts.'
segments.s593-g9: بجستند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb meaning “they sought/sought out.” The following nouns (موبد فرستاده‌ای) are the direct objects/targets of that verb: موبد (a mōbed, Zoroastrian priest) and فرستاده‌ای (“one who was sent,” i.e. a messenger/agent). The sequence lists people they sought; فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite noun formed from the passive/agentive idea of ‘sent one.’
segments.s593-g10: This short phrase gives two coordinated descriptions connected by و: سخن‌گوی (‘speaker’/‘orator’) and بینادل آزاده‌ای (a compound describing the messenger as بینادل — a rare/uncertain word here — and آزاده‌ای, ‘a noble/free-hearted one’). آزاده‌ای is indefinite (the -ای ending) describing the person sought.
segments.s593-g11: کجا is the interrogative adverb ‘where.’ نام here means ‘name’ in the sense of reputation/fame, and آن گو جوانوی is a nominal phrase modifying that name (‘that so‑called young man’); بود is the copula ‘was’ placed after the nominal phrase, so the clause asks where that person’s fame/name was (i.e., where he was known).
segments.s593-g12: This is a predicative clause with two coordinated predicates joined by و. دبیری بزرگ means literally ‘great secretaryship/office’ (used here to indicate a prominent secretary/secretarial rank) and سخن‌گوی is ‘an orator/speaker’; بود is the past copula ‘was,’ tying these qualities to the person just mentioned.
segments.s593-g13: بدان here functions as a dative/goal phrase (classical contraction of به + آن, ‘to that person’ / ‘to him’). تا introduces purpose (‘so that, in order that’), and به نزدیک منذر شود is a verb phrase with به + نزدیک ‘near’ and شود (3rd‑person present/subjunctive of شدن) meaning ‘may become/approach’ — i.e., ‘so that he may come near Munẓir.’
segments.s593-g14: سخن is ‘speech’/‘what is said.’ گوید here is the verb ‘he should speak’ (3rd‑person), but the provided lemma senses do not list the usual verbal sense, so it is flagged below. The coordinating و links the next clause گفت او بشنود: گفت (what is said) + او (he) + بشنود (subjunctive of شنیدن ‘to hear’) — literally ‘and what is said he may hear.’ Overall, the sequence expresses purpose/result: come near so that he may speak and hear what is said.
segments.s593-g15: به منذر بگوید is a ditransitive-style sequence: به + proper name = ‘to Munẓir’, and بگوید (3rd‑person verbal form) reports that he ‘said/told’ (classical narrative use). که‌ای is the classical introducer of direct address where که + ای = ‘that/“O…”’ and the following سرفراز is the vocative ‘O exalted/proud/honoured one.’
segments.s593-g16: This is an optative wish. جهان را uses را as the topical/definite marker (‘the world’). به نام تو = ‘by/in your name’ or ‘under your name.’ بادا is an optative particle meaning ‘may it be,’ and نیاز (‘supplication/Invocation’) is the predicate: ‘May the world’s invocation be in your name,’ i.e. ‘may the whole world invoke your name.’
segments.s593-g57: چو at the start is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. گستاخ شد is a past-tense verbal clause 'became insolent' (شد = became). زو (ز + او) indicates relation toward/of him ('from/of him' / 'toward him') and links who became insolent to the following action. The clause ends with بپرسید شاه, where the past verb بپرسید ('asked') precedes the subject شاه ('the king') — a common classical/poetic word order where verb can come before the subject.
segments.s593-g58: کز ایران begins the direct question with کز = 'from' (here 'from Iran'). چرا is the interrogative 'why'. رنجه گشتی به راه is a verbal phrase meaning 'you went/turned out suffering/on the road' — رنجه = suffer/distress, گشتی is the past/ perfect form of گشتن ('you went/you turned'), and به راه = 'onto the road' or 'took to the road'. The whole string asks why the addressee left Iran and set out in hardship.
segments.s593-g59: فرستاد is the past tense 'sent/dispatched' (here a purposeful dispatch). با او 'with him' indicates accompaniment. یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain' placed before the noun, and پرخرد is an adjective 'very-wise' modifying that person; together they mean 'a certain very-wise man' who was sent.
segments.s593-g60: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause ('who' / 'so that'). او را is the object 'him' marked by the definite object marker را. به نزدیک constructs a directional phrase 'to the near/near the presence of', here followed by the proper name منذر (Munḏir). برد is the past verb 'took / brought' (بردن) — the intended verbal sense is 'took/led', but that specific sense is not among the provided lemma senses.
segments.s593-g61: بگوید here functions as a performative verb meaning 'say'/'tell' (classical narrative uses forms like this to introduce reported speech); the verb form in the given lemma list does not include the verbal sense, so its exact sense is not among the provided senses. که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. آن نامه is 'that letter' and پاسخ نویس is a compound meaning 'the reply (written by) the writer/scribe' — نویس here denotes the person who composes/writes (a scribe/secretary).
segments.s593-g62: به پاسخ is 'as/to the reply' (به marking the relation 'as/to'). سخنهای is the plural 'words/speeches' (sokhanhā-ye). فرخ نویس here names the person whose words are answered; in context this reads 'Farrokh the scribe' (فرخ as a name or epithet and نویس 'scribe/secretary'). The lemma list does not give a proper-name sense for فرخ, so that element is marked accordingly.
segments.s593-g63: وزان پس نگر is a sequence of linkers and an imperative: وزان ('and from that' / 'thereafter'), پس ('then'), نگر (imperative 'look' / 'see'). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here: 'so that / in order that', followed by چه دارد پیام 'what message he has' (چه = what; دارد = 'he has' (present)); پیام = 'message/tidings'.
segments.s593-g64: ازو بشنود is a purposive clause 'so that you/he may hear from him' — ازو = 'from him/of him', بشنود is a present/subjunctive form of شنیدن 'to hear' used here to express the intended reception of news. پاسخ او تمام places the object 'his answer' followed by تمام 'complete' (تمام modifies پاسخ), i.e. 'his full reply.'
segments.s593-g73: چنین گوی is an imperative/formulaic phrase meaning “say thus” (چنین = thus; گوی here is the verb ‘say’ used in an older/poetic form). کاین بد that follows is a content clause introduced by the relative/demonstrative کاین (“this/that which”) + adjective بد (“bad”), and که کرد از نخست is a further subordinate clause: که introduces ‘that,’ کرد is the simple past ‘he did,’ and از نخست is an adverbial phrase ‘from the first/at the beginning.’ Together this segment: “Say thus: that this was bad, that he did from the start….”
segments.s593-g74: که introduces the complement clause (‘that’). بیهوده is an adverb ‘in vain’; پیکار is the noun ‘battle/struggle’ acting as the object of the verbal phrase; بایست is an archaic/modal verb form here meaning ‘was bound to / ought to / took it upon himself’ (sense: must/ought), and جست (here the past of جستن) means ‘sought / sought after’ (to seek). The phrase پیکار بایست جست therefore expresses ‘he sought/fought a battle (and it was) in vain.’
segments.s593-g75: This is a nominal subject + predicate: شهنشاه بهرام گور names the ruler (title + personal name + epithet); ایدرست is an archaic/praise predicate meaning ‘is upright/just’ or an emphatic ‘truly/upright’ (an old poetic copular/predicative form). The whole functions as ‘The king Bahram Gur is upright/just.’
segments.s593-g76: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ‘who/for’ (linking back to the shah). با + noun marks accompaniment or possession: با فر ‘with farr (divine royal glory)’; و coordinates; برزست is an archaic predicate form (برز-ست) meaning ‘is exalted/was exalted’ (the -ست element is the old copula). و با لشکرست parallels the previous construction: ‘and with an army he is/has an army.’ Overall: ‘who is endowed with farr and exaltation, and who has an army.’
segments.s593-g77: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’; سوراخ is the noun ‘hole’; چون مار is a simile ‘like a snake’; بیرون is the adverb ‘out’; کشید is the past tense of کشیدن ‘to pull/draw/drag out’ — in poetic word order the simile intervenes, but the clause means ‘he drew himself out of the hole like a snake.’
segments.s593-g78: همی is an adverb often marking iterative/continuous or immediate action (here roughly ‘then/so’); دامن خویش is the possessed noun phrase ‘his own hem/skirt’ where خویش is the reflexive possessive formed from خوی + clitic ش; در خون is a prepositional phrase ‘in/through blood’; and کشید again is the past ‘he dragged/dragged along.’ The clause: ‘he dragged his own hem through blood.’
segments.s593-g79: گر is the conditional conjunction ‘if’; ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning roughly ‘in that place/at that time’ or ‘in that case’; من بودمی is the counterfactual past first-person form ‘I had been / I would have been’ (بودمی is an archaic subjunctive/conditional form); رای زن is ‘رای’ (counsel/plan/opinion) plus the light verb زدن; together رای زن = ‘to give/offer counsel’ (here: ‘I would have given the advice’).
segments.s593-g80: به ایرانیان is the indirect object ‘to/for the Iranians’; بر is the preposition ‘against/upon’; نبودی is the negative past/counterfactual form (نبودی = ‘would not have been’/‘was not’) built on بود; شکن is a noun ‘breaker/tormentor/oppressor.’ The clause thus states the content of the advice: ‘that there would have been no oppressor against the Iranians.’
segments.s593-g65: This is two simple past actions in sequence: بیامد (past 3sg) ‘came’ followed by سخنها بگفت, where سخنها is the plural object ‘words/sayings’ and بگفت is the past verb ‘said/spoke’ with the classical preverb b-. The fused form جوانو = جوانِ او / جوان و functions as ‘the youth’ (topic/addressee omitted) and the clause order is Verb–Subject/Topic–Object in compressed poetic style.
segments.s593-g66: رخ منذر is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the face of Munḏir' (منذر as a personal name). از رای او is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of his counsel/opinion' modifying the verb. برشکفت is a past, 3rd-person singular verbal form (بر + شکفت) meaning 'burst open' or 'lit up' and completes the clause.
segments.s593-g67: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is past 3sg 'he heard'. زان + noun supplies the source/preceding reference 'from that'. The phrase مرد بنا is an inverted attributive (مرد (بنا)) where بنا functions as an epithet ('builder' here) attached to مرد; سخن is the object 'speech/words' — together: 'when he heard that man's (the builder's) speech.'
segments.s593-g68: مر is the dative/clitic meaning 'to me' (archaic). آن نامه را پاسخ is a noun phrase with را marking the definite object ('the reply to that letter'), and افگند is past 3sg 'threw/cast'. بن here functions as a locative/adverbial noun 'to the ground/base' (poetic usage) attached after the verb.
segments.s593-g69: جوانوی را گفت uses را after جوانوی to mark the addressee/topic ('to the young man he said'); گفت here introduces direct speech. کای is the vocative particle 'O' and پرخرد is an epithet 'very wise' addressing the young man. Word order is verb + indirect object/topic + quoted vocative.
segments.s593-g70: هرانکس که ... is a free relative: 'whoever (هرانکس) who (که) ...'. کرد is past 3sg 'did', forming the protasis 'whoever did bad (بد کرد)'; the apodosis is کیفر برد, where کیفر is 'punishment' and برد here is a classical verb meaning 'to take/receive' (past), so 'will/has borne punishment' — a general proverb-like statement in past form used for general truth in poetry.
segments.s593-g71: شنیدم is first-person past 'I heard'. The sequence همه هرچ دادی پیام is an inverted noun phrase: همه هرچ 'all/whatever' + دادی پیام 'the message you gave' — دادی is 2nd-person past 'you gave' governing پیام as its object; English order is restored in translation.
segments.s593-g72: وزان is a conjunctive prepositional adverb 'and from that / and of those', linking what follows to the previous clause. نامداران is a plural noun 'renowned/illustrious ones'; که کردی سلام is a relative clause 'whom you greeted' (که = 'whom/that', کردی = 2sg past 'you did', سلام = 'greeting'). The whole phrase modifies what was heard.
segments.s593-g41: بگوی is an imperative/hortative form (classical/poetic) meaning 'tell' (literally 'say'); it takes the following clause as its object. این‌که introduces a content clause 'that which...' and گفتی is a past second-person verb 'you said' describing the content of that clause. به + بهرامشاه marks the indirect object/recipient 'to Bahramshah'. Word order is verb — clause/object (typical in poetic Persian), so the whole segment = 'Say that which you said to Bahramshah.'
segments.s593-g42: چو here is conditional/temporal ('if' or 'when'); پاسخ is the noun 'answer/reply' and بجویی is the present/subjunctive 2nd person singular of 'to seek' ('you seek'). نمایدت is a 3rd-person form with an enclitic object ('it shows you' or 'it would show you') — in context it functions as 'it will show you'. راه is the object of نمایدت ('the way/path'). The clause therefore means 'when/if you seek an answer, it will show you the way.'
segments.s593-g43: فرستاد is simple past 'he sent/dispatched'. با او 'with him' shows accompaniment. یکی نامدار is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/one renowned (man)' — یکی here = 'a certain' and نامدار = 'renowned'. The phrase functions as the direct object of فرستاد: 'he sent a renowned one with him.'
segments.s593-g44: جوانوی is an adjectival/noun form 'young man/youngly' describing the person; شد is the past 'became/went' (here stative or inchoative 'became / came to be'). تا has a directional/extent sense 'as far as / to' (classical use meaning 'to/unto'), so تا در شهریار gives the destination/position 'to/in the presence of the king' (شهریار). Together: 'he (the young/young man) went/was placed before the king.'
segments.s593-g45: چو = 'when'. بهرام را uses را as the definite-object marker on 'Bahram'. دید is the past 'saw'. داننده مرد (literally 'knowing man') is an appositional noun phrase placed after the verb in poetic inversion and functions as the subject ('the knowing man'). The order is somewhat inverted for verse: 'When the knowing man saw Bahram...'.
segments.s593-g46: برو here functions as a verbal/adverbial element 'going / upon approaching' (not an imperative) — a common poetic use where a motion-verb introduces a circumstantial action. آفریننده را is the direct object 'the Creator' with را. یاد کرد is a compound past meaning 'remembered' or 'called to mind/mentioned' (کرد is the light verb). So the clause means 'approaching him, he remembered the Creator.'
segments.s593-g47: ازان = 'from that' (from + demonstrative). A coordinated list follows: برز و بالا 'height and loftiness' (two close nouns/adjectives), و آن یال و کفت 'and that mane and palm' — classical/Persian poetic enumeration where multiple nouns are linked with و. The whole phrase supplies the cause/source or the items from which the following state arose.
segments.s593-g48: فروماند is a past/passive-like verbal form 'was left behind / became exhausted / remained'. بینادل is a rare/uncertain word (textual/lexical difficulty noted in the manuscript) and here functions inside the predicate (its exact nuance uncertain). اندر = 'in/within', شگفت = 'wonder/astonishment' (used as a noun). Together the segment expresses that he remained/fell back, left in wonder/amazement.

sentences.s593-s1: When the Iranians learned the news, each one hastened toward a remedy.
sentences.s593-s2: When, from that suffering, they became utterly weary, they sat down together, one with another, as companions.
sentences.s593-s3: for this deed surpassed all measure — from Rum and from India and the horsemen of the plain.
sentences.s593-s4: One remedy must now be devised: to devote heart and life to this task.
sentences.s593-s9: May you be the guardian of Iran, its ardour within you; be the support of the brave in every place.
sentences.s593-s10: When this throne became without a king and without a crown, the frontier, from blood, became like a rider's plume.
sentences.s593-s11: We proclaim you lord of the frontier, for we have seen that this border's worth is because of you.
sentences.s593-s12: Now plunder comes from you and blood is spilled, pillage and hanging in every place.
sentences.s593-s25: It keeps dripping, as if from her face; the scent of musk keeps coming from her hair.
sentences.s593-s26: The speaker became without rank and unconscious; his message was entirely forgotten.
sentences.s593-s27: Bahram perceived it and stood transfixed; from that sight his eye and his heart grew dark.
sentences.s593-s28: He asked many questions and struck him; then, kindly, he set him upon the throne.
sentences.s593-s41: He saw the king's youthful face, and also heard from him a few words.
sentences.s593-s42: He asked (it) so that perhaps he might obtain the throne, greatness, victory, and fortune.
sentences.s593-s43: When he heard such words from Munẓir, he cast one clear thought to the foundation.
sentences.s593-s44: He answered thus: 'O noble one, in wisdom you need no one.'
sentences.s593-s45: If wisdom was gotten from the Iranians, many of the freemen were slain.
sentences.s593-s46: Now I, a certain old seeker of renown, (if you listen) will speak a tale.
sentences.s593-s47: You, with the king Bahram Gūr, must stride proudly in azure war and ardor.
sentences.s593-s48: In the land of Iran, in the aba with cheetah and falcon, just so was the proud-necked king.
sentences.s593-s49: Truly, hearing the Iranians' words should bring no harm from any agitation.
sentences.s593-s50: You should also say whatever is fitting—wisdom and keeping away from the foolish.
sentences.s593-s51: Keep your behavior far from that counsel; turn away from obloquy and reproach.
sentences.s593-s52: When Munḏir heard, he gave him a gift; someone from the prosperous city made him glad.
sentences.s593-s17: The wise young man came forward from before the chiefs toward the plain of spearmen.
sentences.s593-s18: He spoke to Munḏir and gave a letter; he recalled the words of the Iranians.
sentences.s593-s19: The Arab king heard his words; to answer he did not open his lips at all.
sentences.s593-s20: Thus he said: “O learned, seeker of a remedy, speak this message to the Shahanshah.”
sentences.s593-s13: Before this you were not an evildoer; you feared curse and reproach.
sentences.s593-s14: Look at these until they please you; in the elders' judgment this will be profitable to you.
sentences.s593-s15: Except for you, judgment is otherwise; for his thought is superior to the superior ones.
sentences.s593-s16: The messenger told what he had seen, and also the words he had heard from the experts.
sentences.s593-s5: They sought a mobad and a messenger — an eloquent, noble-hearted man.
sentences.s593-s6: Where was the fame of that so‑called youth? He was a great secretary and an eloquent speaker.
sentences.s593-s7: They told him to go near Munẓir so that he might speak and hear what was said.
sentences.s593-s8: He told Munẓir, “O noble one — may the whole world invoke your name.”
sentences.s593-s29: When he became insolent, the king asked him, "Why have you left Iran and taken to the road in hardship?"
sentences.s593-s30: He sent a certain very-wise man with him, who took him before Munḏir.
sentences.s593-s31: Say that that letter is the reply to the words of Farrokh the scribe.
sentences.s593-s32: And then look to see what message he has, so that you may hear his complete answer from him.
sentences.s593-s37: Say this: that the bad thing he did from the first was to seek battle in vain.
sentences.s593-s38: The shah Bahram Gur is upright — he is endowed with farr and exaltation, and he has an army.
sentences.s593-s39: From the hole, like a snake, he thrust himself out; he dragged his own hem through blood.
sentences.s593-s40: If I had been there, I would have advised that there would have been no oppressor against the Iranians.
sentences.s593-s33: The youth came and spoke his words; Munḏir's face, from his thought, burst open.
sentences.s593-s34: When he heard that man's speech, he cast the reply to that letter to me.
sentences.s593-s35: He said to the young man, 'O very-wise one: whoever did evil will receive punishment.'
sentences.s593-s36: I heard everything you said: the message you gave and the greetings you offered to those renowned men.
sentences.s593-s21: Tell (him) that which you told Bahramshah, for if you seek an answer it will show you the way.
sentences.s593-s22: He sent with him a certain renowned young man who went/entered before the king.
sentences.s593-s23: When the knowing man saw Bahram, approaching him he remembered the Creator.
sentences.s593-s24: From that height and loftiness — and that mane and palm — he was left behind, astonished.
